macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Position on Egyptian and Edomite Women: Tradition versus Logic

micro_segments.title: Review of Rabbi Shimon's Argument for Permitting Egyptian and Edomite Women
Rabbis' Counterargument Using Challal and Arayot as Precedent
Rabbi Shimon's Response: Kareis Cases Are Different
Rabbis Cite Rabbi Elazar ben Yaakov on Issur Asei Creating Challal Status
Rabbi Shimon's Final Position: I Have a Halakha Tradition
Tosafot's Analysis: Tradition Versus Reasoned Debate

macro_segments.title: The Baraita Debate: Egyptian and Edomite Women and the Textual Support

micro_segments.title: Baraita: Rabbi Shimon's Tradition and Textual Support from 'Banim v'Lo Banot'
Rabbi Yehuda's Alternative Reading: Focus on 'Yuladu' Implies Mothers Are Forbidden
Rabbi Yochanan's Assessment: Without Yehuda's Insight, He Would Have No Standing

macro_segments.title: The Marriage Problem for Mixed Generations: Applying Yehuda's Gerim Principle

micro_segments.title: Yehuda's Position: Gerim Are Considered Kahal and Cannot Marry Restricted Nations
The Logical Problem: If Women Are Permitted, Whom Can Males Marry?
Proposed Resolution: Third Generation Must Come from Permitted Unions
Gemara's Counterargument: B'di'avad Scenario Doesn't Appear in Torah
Mamzer Analogy: Torah Does Address Consequences of Transgressions
Distinction: Torah Teaches Negative Consequences, Not Positive Permissions from Sin
Mechzir Grushato Counterexample: Torah Does Teach Heteirim Derived from Prohibitions
Gemara's Resolution: That Pasuk's Primary Point Is Issur; Hetter Is Secondary
Conclusion: Third Generation Mitzri Requires a Mutar Path; Women Must Be Forbidden

macro_segments.title: Textual Analysis of 'Banim,' 'Dorot,' and 'Lahem' in the Pasuk

micro_segments.title: Redundancy Question: Why Say Both 'Banim' and 'Dorot' If They Mean the Same Thing?
Explanation of 'Banim': Generation Must Count from Them, Not from Har Sinai
Explanation of 'Dorot': Three Generations, Not Three Children
First 'Lahem': Counting Begins with the Converts, Not the Fetuses
Second 'Lahem': The Psul Follows Them Regardless of Mother or Father
Mamzer Parallel: Learning Similar Rules from Mamzer's 'Lo' and 'Lahem'

macro_segments.title: Dispute Over Whether Psul Follows Father, Mother, or Weightier Status

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan via Rabba bar bar Chana: Mitzri Sheini Marrying Mitzrit Rishona Has Third Generation Child
Implication: Status Follows the Father
Rav Yosef's Challenge: Minymin's Example Shows Both Parents' Status Matters
Minymin's Strategy: First Generation Marries First Generation; Son Will Marry Second Generation
Rabbi Yochanan's Response: Emend the Baraita to Say Even First Generation Works
Rav Dimi's Tradition: Mitzri Sheini Marrying Mitzrit Rishona Has Sheini Child
Abaye's Challenge: This Contradicts Rabbi Yochanan's Teaching on Fetal Status
Chatas Pregnant Offering: Evidence That Fetus Is Independent Entity
Ubar Yerech Imo: Fetus in Womb Has Separate Identity
Abaye's Logic: If Fetus Is Independent, Status Should Follow Father
Rav Dimi's Response: The Pasuk Focuses on Birth; Therefore Mother's Status Applies Here
Rav Dimi Confirms: Normally You Follow the Father; Here It's Special Because of 'Yuladu'

macro_segments.title: Ruling on Mixed Marriages: When Both Converts Are Not Jewish, Follow the Father

micro_segments.title: Ba'umot Halech Achar Hazachar: Among Non-Jews, Status Follows the Male
Source: Canaanite Woman with Non-Canaanite Man; Child Is Not Canaanite
Nitgeiru Halech Achar Pagum Shebishneihem: After Conversion, Follow the Worse Status
Example: Mitzri Marrying Amonis; If Male Child, He's Amoni; If Female, She's Mitzrit Sheiniya

macro_segments.title: Mamzerim and Netinim: Permanent and Absolute Prohibitions

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Mamzerim and Netinim Are Forbidden Forever, Both Males and Females
Resh Lakish's Novel Position: Female Mamzer Becomes Permitted After Ten Generations
Gezeira Shavah: Learning 'Ad Olam' from Ammoni and Moavi's Pasuk
Don Minah Uminha vs. Don Minah Ve'okei Be'atrah: Two Approaches to Applying Gezeira Shavah
Resolution: Ten Generations Apply to All; Thereafter Only Males Are Forbidden Forever
Rabbi Eliezer's Cryptic Answer: If I Had a Third Generation, I Would Purify Them
Interpretation: Rabbi Eliezer Holds Mamzerim Don't Live Beyond a Few Generations
Rabbi Zeira's Nuance: Known Mamzerim Live; Unknown Mamzerim Don't
Story of Rav Ami: Announcing Mamzer Status Gives the Person Life
